ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for palak paneer with tandoori roti as a combined dish; mapped to parent class 'curry dish' as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Spinach dish — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for palak paneer (spinach-based curry); roti component not separately coded in this composite entry.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for palak paneer with tandoori roti as a composite dish. A0CKP (spinach dish) is the closest defensible anchor for the dominant curry component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Indian dish.

serving.description: 1 plate (palak paneer with one tandoori roti)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 200 g of palak paneer curry and one medium tandoori roti (~100 g), as commonly served in Singapore Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Palak Paneer with Tandoori Roti — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for this composite dish (cr_id G00010520) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from the HPB record. Saturated fat (4.2 g/100g) is derived from the HPB record's fat profile for this dish type; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Chapati / whole wheat flatbread GI analogous estimate — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for palak paneer with tandoori roti as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on: (1) whole wheat tandoori roti is analogous to whole wheat chapati (GI ~62, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021); (2) palak paneer curry component has negligible GI impact due to high protein (7.05 g/100g), dietary fibre (2.8 g/100g), and fat (7.59 g/100g) which attenuate glycaemic response. Composite dish GI weighted toward roti component. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (62 × 9.86 × 3) / 100 = 18.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 311 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 247 mg/100g and calcium 235 mg/100g (DASH-diet minerals)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt and butter in preparation; the potassium and calcium content provides partial DASH-diet benefit, but a 300 g serving delivers approximately 933 mg sodium, which represents 40–47% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg, so portion control and low-sodium accompaniments are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 9.86 g/100g with dietary fibre 2.8 g/100g and protein 7.05 g/100g providing glycaemic buffering; composite dish GI estimated at 62 (medium)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to one tandoori roti and a moderate serving of palak paneer, as the high fibre and protein content attenuate postprandial glucose rise, but the medium GI of the whole wheat roti component warrants monitoring of post-meal blood glucose; request reduced cream and butter to manage caloric density and insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 4.2 g/100g from paneer, cream, and butter; cholesterol 19 mg/100g; sodium 311 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with reduced cream and butter to lower saturated fat intake; the spinach component provides beneficial dietary nitrates and potassium supporting vascular health, and the dietary fibre (2.8 g/100g) contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction, but overall saturated fat and sodium levels warrant moderation in serving size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric/curcumin, ginger) and antioxidant-rich spinach; nightshade ingredients (tomato) may concern sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric and ginger in garam masala and the antioxidants in spinach; however, those with known nightshade sensitivity should request tomato-free preparation, and reduced cream is advised to limit saturated fat approaching the 5 g/100g threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Palak Paneer with Tandoori Roti is a classic North Indian vegetarian meal consisting of two components: palak paneer, a creamy spinach-based curry made with fresh paneer (Indian cottage cheese), and tandoori roti, a whole wheat flatbread baked in a clay tandoor oven. The palak paneer is prepared by blanching and puréeing fresh spinach, then simmering it with sautéed onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, and aromatic spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and garam masala, finished with cream and butter to create a rich, velvety texture. The tandoori roti is made from whole wheat atta dough, shaped into rounds, and baked at high heat in a tandoor, producing a slightly charred, chewy flatbread with a smoky flavour. The combined dish delivers 130 kcal per 100 g with 7.05 g protein, 7.59 g fat, and 9.86 g carbohydrate. Notably, calcium is high at 235 mg/100g from paneer, iron at 1.74 mg/100g from spinach, magnesium at 62 mg/100g, and potassium at 247 mg/100g. Dietary fibre at 2.8 g/100g comes from both spinach and whole wheat roti. Sodium is 311 mg/100g, reflecting typical restaurant preparation. This dish is widely consumed in Singapore at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. It is suitable for vegetarians and can be made halal. Clinically, the high protein and fibre content provide glycaemic buffering for Type 2 Diabetes patients, while the DASH-diet minerals (potassium, calcium) partially offset the moderate sodium load relevant to hypertension management. The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger are of interest for rheumatoid arthritis. Saturated fat from cream and paneer is a consideration for cardiovascular disease patients.
common_user_queries: Calories in palak paneer with roti
Is palak paneer good for diabetes?
Is palak paneer high in protein?
Can I eat palak paneer with hypertension?
What is tandoori roti made of?

ingredient_synonyms.saag: leafy greens (often used interchangeably with palak in restaurant menus)
ingredient_synonyms.tandoori roti: whole wheat flatbread baked in tandoor
ingredient_synonyms.ghee: clarified butter (sometimes used instead of butter)

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010520, confidence 0.95) which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using whole wheat chapati as the closest defensible proxy, clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite palak paneer with tandoori roti dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010520) as of 2026. Saturated fat value of 4.2 g/100g is estimated from the HPB fat profile for this dish type based on known composition of paneer, cream, and butter components; this is consistent with the input record's health_suitability notes referencing 4.2 g/100g saturated fat. The food_group in the input record was listed as 'Grains and staples / Breads and flatbreads' which reflects the roti component only; this has been reclassified to 'Mixed / Curry with flatbread' to accurately represent the composite dish. The dish name in the input contained '❤️ Free' which appears to be a promotional or menu-style label; the canonical food name has been normalized to 'Palak Paneer with Tandoori Roti'. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on whole wheat chapati from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
